title: Your First Commit
intro: A commit is like a save point in a video game. It captures a snapshot of your project at a specific moment. Let's create your very first one.

teach.title: Commits = Save Points
teach.body: In a video game, you save your progress so you can come back to that exact spot if something goes wrong. A **commit** in Git works the same way -- it's a snapshot of your entire project at one moment in time. You can always go back to any save point.

teach.title: The 3-Step Workflow
teach.body:
Making a commit is a three-step process:

1. **Stage** -- Pick which files you want to include in this save point (`git add`)
2. **Commit** -- Take the snapshot with a short message describing what changed (`git commit`)
3. **Push** -- Upload your save point to GitHub so it's backed up online (`git push`)

For now, we'll focus on steps 1 and 2.

teach.title: The Commands You'll Use
teach.code:
git init                          # Start tracking this folder
git add .                         # Stage all files
git status                        # See what's staged
git commit -m "Initial commit"    # Save the snapshot
teach.annotation: Don't worry about memorizing these -- you'll practice each one in the exercise.

teach.body: A commit is a save point for your project -- stage your changes with `git add`, then save them with `git commit -m "your message"`.

steps.instruction: First, initialize a new Git repository in this folder. This tells Git to start tracking changes here.
steps.hint: The command is 'git' followed by a short word meaning 'initialize' or 'start'.

steps.instruction: Now add both files to the staging area. The staging area is like a shopping cart -- you pick what you want to include before checking out.
steps.hint: Use 'git add' followed by a dot (.) to add everything in the current folder.

steps.instruction: Check the status to see what's staged and ready to commit.
steps.output:
On branch main

No commits yet

Changes to be committed:
  (use "git rm --cached <file>..." to unstage)

	new file:   index.html
	new file:   style.css
steps.hint: The command is 'git status' -- it shows the current state of your working directory.

steps.instruction: Everything looks good. Now save this snapshot with a descriptive message.
steps.output:
[main (root-commit) a1b2c3d] Initial commit
 2 files changed, 0 insertions(+), 0 deletions(-)
 create mode 100644 index.html
 create mode 100644 style.css
steps.hint: Use 'git commit -m' followed by your message in quotes. Try something like "Initial commit".

exercise.completionMessage: You just made your first commit! This save point is now permanently recorded in your Git history. You can always come back to this exact state of your project.
